questions.question_text: A licensee is asked to help a client purchase vacant desert land but has only sold residential homes. What should the licensee do?
questions.correct_answer: Disclose their limited experience with vacant land
distractors: Decline the transaction entirely and refer out
Proceed without disclosing any limitation at all
Refer the client to ADRE for further assistance
questions.explanation: Arizona requires licensees to disclose experience and knowledge limitations. The licensee can still handle the transaction but must inform the client about their limited experience with vacant land sales.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is considered a niche area of real estate under Arizona's exam content?
questions.correct_answer: Timeshare sales
distractors: Standard residential resale homes
Residential rental leasing
Mortgage loan origination
questions.explanation: The Arizona exam recognizes several niche areas including new home sales, property management, timeshare, vacant land, and commercial real estate. Standard residential sales is the default practice area, not a niche.

questions.question_text: A licensee sells new homes on behalf of a builder. What is required for this activity?
questions.correct_answer: A license and a designated broker are required
distractors: A special builder's representative permit only
Only the builder needs to hold a real estate license
No license is needed for new construction sales
questions.explanation: Licensees selling new homes on behalf of builders must hold an active real estate license and work under a designated broker, just like any other real estate sales activity.

questions.question_text: A licensee lists a property with a septic system in a rural area. What should the licensee recommend?
questions.correct_answer: An inspection to verify ADEQ compliance
distractors: No inspection since rural land is exempt
Only a well water test for the property
A full environmental Phase II assessment
questions.explanation: Alternative wastewater systems (septic, aerobic treatment) are common in rural Arizona and must meet ADEQ standards. Licensees should recommend inspections to verify the system is compliant and functioning properly.

questions.question_text: Which type of property typically requires an Affidavit of Disclosure in Arizona?
questions.correct_answer: Unsubdivided vacant land of 5 or fewer parcels
distractors: All residential homes valued over $500,000
Commercial properties with existing tenants
Condominiums located in planned communities
questions.explanation: The Affidavit of Disclosure is specifically required for unsubdivided land sales (5 or fewer parcels). It addresses property conditions, water adequacy, and other factors important for rural and vacant land.

questions.question_text: Is a real estate license required to manage property for others in Arizona?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, a license is required for compensated PM
distractors: No, property management is fully exempt
Only if managing more than 10 rental units
Only the property owner needs to be licensed
questions.explanation: Arizona requires a real estate license to manage property for others for compensation. The designated broker of the PM firm is responsible for all property management activities.

questions.question_text: A property manager receives a $3,000 security deposit on Monday. By when must it be deposited into the trust account?
questions.correct_answer: Within 3 banking days
distractors: Within 24 hours of receipt
Within 5 business days
By the end of the same day
questions.explanation: All trust funds, including security deposits and rental income, must be deposited into the trust account within 3 banking days of receipt. This applies to all licensees handling client funds.

questions.question_text: A broker keeps $1,000 of personal funds in the trust account to cover bank fees. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, up to $1,000 for bank fees is allowed
distractors: No, any personal funds constitute commingling
Yes, up to $5,000 is permitted by the rules
Only with prior written approval from ADRE
questions.explanation: Arizona allows a broker to maintain a reasonable amount of personal funds (up to $1,000) in the trust account to cover bank service charges. Amounts beyond this constitute commingling.

questions.question_text: How long must trust account records be maintained under Arizona rules?
questions.correct_answer: 5 years
distractors: 3 years
7 years
10 years
questions.explanation: Trust account records must be maintained for 5 years, consistent with the general document retention requirement in the Commissioner's rules. ADRE can audit these records at any time.

questions.question_text: ADRE auditors arrive at a brokerage unannounced to review trust accounts. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, ADRE can audit trust accounts at any time
distractors: No, 30 days of written notice is required first
Only with a court-issued warrant for the records
Only during previously scheduled annual reviews
questions.explanation: ADRE conducts both regular and random audits of brokerage trust accounts. Auditors can verify proper handling of trust funds, record-keeping accuracy, and timely deposits without prior notice.

questions.question_text: What is the maximum security deposit a landlord can charge in Arizona?
questions.correct_answer: One and a half months' rent
distractors: One full month's rent only
Two full months' rent only
Three full months' rent only
questions.explanation: Under the Arizona Residential Landlord and Tenant Act (ARLTA), security deposits are capped at 1.5 months' rent. There is no distinction between furnished and unfurnished properties for this limit.

questions.question_text: A tenant moves out of their apartment. Within what time frame must the landlord return the security deposit?
questions.correct_answer: 14 business days with an itemized statement
distractors: 30 calendar days with no statement required
7 business days with an itemized deduction list
21 calendar days with written notice to tenant
questions.explanation: Arizona landlords must return the security deposit within 14 business days of move-out, accompanied by an itemized statement of any deductions. Failing to do so can result in the landlord forfeiting the right to withhold.

questions.question_text: A landlord wants to enter a tenant's unit to make non-emergency repairs. How much notice is required?
questions.correct_answer: 2 days written notice
distractors: 24 hours verbal notice
48 hours written notice
1 week written notice
questions.explanation: Arizona landlords must provide 2 days written notice before entering a rental unit, except in emergencies. This protects the tenant's right to quiet enjoyment of the premises.

questions.question_text: Which law governs residential rental properties statewide in Arizona?
questions.correct_answer: Arizona Residential Landlord and Tenant Act
distractors: Arizona Property Management Licensing Statute
ARS Title 32 Rental Regulation Provisions
Arizona Housing Standards and Compliance Act
questions.explanation: The Arizona Residential Landlord and Tenant Act (ARLTA), found in ARS Title 33 Chapter 10, governs residential rental properties statewide. It does not apply to commercial leases.

questions.question_text: A landlord charges a non-refundable pet fee clearly disclosed in the lease. Is this permitted in Arizona?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, non-refundable fees are allowed if disclosed
distractors: No, all tenant charges must be fully refundable
Only if the pet fee is under $200 per animal
Only with prior written approval from ADRE first
questions.explanation: Arizona permits non-refundable fees (like pet fees) as long as they are properly disclosed in the lease agreement. However, non-refundable deposits are not allowed — deposits must be refundable.

questions.question_text: A tenant fails to pay rent. How many days does the landlord's notice give the tenant to pay or vacate?
questions.correct_answer: 5 days
distractors: 3 days
10 days
14 days
questions.explanation: For nonpayment of rent, Arizona requires a 5-day notice to pay or vacate. If the tenant does not pay within 5 days, the landlord can proceed with a formal eviction (forcible detainer action) through the courts.

questions.question_text: A tenant's unit has a health and safety violation. What type of notice does the landlord provide?
questions.correct_answer: A 10-day notice
distractors: A 5-day notice
A 30-day notice
A 3-day notice
questions.explanation: Health and safety violations require a 10-day notice. This is shorter than the 30-day notice for general lease violations but longer than the 5-day nonpayment notice, reflecting the urgency of health/safety concerns.

questions.question_text: A landlord changes the locks while the tenant is at work to force them out. This is:
questions.correct_answer: Illegal self-help eviction
distractors: Permitted after a 5-day notice
Legal if the tenant owes back rent
Allowed with 24 hours verbal notice
questions.explanation: Self-help eviction (changing locks, removing possessions, shutting off utilities) is illegal in Arizona. Landlords must go through the formal court eviction process (forcible detainer action) regardless of the reason.

questions.question_text: A tenant's plumbing fails and the landlord ignores written repair requests for 2 weeks. What remedy does the tenant have?
questions.correct_answer: Repair and deduct up to $300 or half month's rent
distractors: Withhold rent for 90 days without giving notice
Immediately terminate the lease without warning
File a lawsuit only after waiting 60 full days
questions.explanation: Under ARLTA, if a landlord fails to address habitability issues after written notice, the tenant can repair and deduct costs (up to $300 or half month's rent), withhold rent, or terminate the lease.

questions.question_text: Arizona law regarding local rent control ordinances provides that:
questions.correct_answer: State law preempts local rent control
distractors: Cities may enact their own rent caps
Rent control applies in Phoenix and Tucson
Only counties can impose rent limitations
questions.explanation: Arizona state law preempts local rent control — municipalities cannot impose rent caps or rent stabilization measures. Landlords can raise rent to any amount with proper notice (30 days for month-to-month tenancies).
